1.synthesis: The Feast of Unleavened Bread approaches; the chief priests and scribes are seeking to put Jesus to death but fear the people (Luke 22:1–2). Then Satan enters Judas Iscariot, one of the Twelve (Luke 22:3). Judas approaches the chief priests and officers and agrees to hand Jesus over for money; they are glad and arrange the payment (Luke 22:4–5). He seeks an opportunity to hand him over in the absence of the crowd (Luke 22:6).
1.historical_context: The Feast of Unleavened Bread (Hag HaMatzot) began on Nisan 15 and lasted seven days; it was celebrated together with Passover, which fell on Nisan 14. Jerusalem's population swelled by tens of thousands during the festival, making crowd dynamics a genuine political concern for the authorities. Luke's identification of Satan entering Judas is his interpretation of the betrayal's cosmic dimension—it does not eliminate Judas's moral agency (he 'went away and conferred,' v.4) but places the betrayal within the larger conflict between Jesus and the adversary that has run throughout the Gospel since the temptation (Luke 4:1–13). The 'convenient opportunity in the absence of the crowd' echoes the same crowd-protection that had prevented the authorities from arresting Jesus in the temple.
1.literary_structure: The brief passage serves as the passion narrative's opening—the agreement that sets everything in motion. Luke identifies three agents: the religious authorities (institutional plotting), Satan (cosmic adversary), and Judas (human complicity). This tripartite causation is consistent with Luke's understanding of the passion as simultaneously human crime, satanic opposition, and divine purpose (cf. Acts 2:23: 'delivered up according to the definite plan of God... killed by the hands of lawless men'). The passage frames the entire passion narrative as a battle in which these forces converge on the Son of Man.
1.theological_application: Judas's betrayal stands as a permanent warning against the mystery of apostasy—the one who walked with Jesus for years, saw his miracles, heard his teaching, nonetheless became the instrument of his death. The identification of Satan's entry does not exonerate Judas; it names the spiritual reality of what happens when a disciple opens himself to the adversary's suggestion rather than resisting. The church must hold together the realities of human responsibility and cosmic warfare without collapsing one into the other. The fact that Jesus knew what was coming (Luke 22:21–22) and went forward anyway is the measure of his resolve to accomplish the Father's purpose regardless of human treachery.
1.christology: The passion narrative begins with Satan's entrance into Judas—picking up the thread from Luke 4:13 where the tempter 'departed from him until an opportune time.' The opportune time (kairos) has come: the decisive eschatological battle will be fought at Golgotha. Jesus does not resist the betrayal because the cross is not defeat but the way through which Satan's own power is broken (Colossians 2:15). The one whom the adversary uses to destroy the Son of God becomes, through the resurrection, the proof that the adversary has been defeated by the very event he orchestrated.

7.synthesis: Jesus sends Peter and John ahead to prepare the Passover, directing them to a man carrying a water jar who will lead them to the room (Luke 22:7–12). At the table, Jesus speaks with intense desire: 'I have earnestly desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer' (Luke 22:15). He takes the cup and gives thanks, then distributes bread ('This is my body, given for you; do this in remembrance of me') and after supper the cup ('This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood') (Luke 22:17–20). He announces the betrayal: the hand of the one who betrays him is on the table, and the Son of Man goes as it is determined, but woe to that man (Luke 22:21–22). The disciples question which of them it could be (Luke 22:23).
7.historical_context: The Passover meal commemorated the exodus from Egypt, recalling the blood on the doorposts that protected Israel from the angel of death (Exodus 12:1–14). Its sequence included four cups of wine, explanatory words over unleavened bread, retelling of the exodus story, and psalms of praise. Jesus' adaptation transforms the Passover framework: the bread and cup point not backward to the exodus but forward to his death as the new exodus (Luke 9:31 calls the crucifixion his 'departure,' exodos). The 'new covenant in my blood' (Luke 22:20) explicitly fulfills Jeremiah 31:31–34's promise of a covenant written on the heart, ratified not by animal blood but by the blood of the Son.
7.literary_structure: The passage moves from preparation (vv.7–13) to table discourse (vv.14–23). Luke's eucharistic account includes two cups (vv.17, 20)—the first before the bread, the second after—which some scholars connect to the Passover sequence. The 'do this in remembrance of me' command (v.19) is Luke's unique contribution over Mark's account, making the Eucharist explicitly an ongoing practice of the community rather than a one-time act. The betrayal announcement (vv.21–23) intrudes immediately after the institution—a deliberately jarring juxtaposition: the covenant body and blood are given, and the hand of the betrayer is at that same table.
7.theological_application: The Last Supper is the founding act of the church's central sacrament—the meal that week after week rehearses the body given and blood poured out 'for you.' Luke's 'do this in remembrance of me' frames the Eucharist as an act of anamnesis: not merely mental recollection but participatory re-entry into the reality of Christ's giving. The communal context—disciples gathered, bread and cup shared—means that every celebration is also a declaration of the community's identity as those who live by the body and blood of the one who gave himself. The presence of the betrayer at the table is a sobering reminder that the meal is always received by those who are capable of profound failure.
7.christology: Jesus' 'I have earnestly desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer' (Luke 22:15) reveals the depth of his intentional self-giving: he goes to the cross not as a passive victim but as one who has longed to make this offering. 'My body given for you' and 'my blood... the new covenant'—these phrases make Jesus simultaneously the priest who officiates, the sacrifice offered, and the covenant mediator who ratifies the new relationship. He is the Passover lamb whose blood protects; the bread of life whose body is given; the mediator of the covenant that Jeremiah promised. The Eucharist is the weekly proclamation that this single person encompasses all these roles in himself.

24.synthesis: A dispute breaks out among the disciples about which of them is the greatest (Luke 22:24). Jesus redefines greatness: Gentile kings lord it over their subjects, but among the disciples the greatest must be as the youngest and the leader as the one who serves. 'For who is the greater, one who reclines at table or one who serves? Is it not the one who reclines? But I am among you as the one who serves' (Luke 22:25–27). He affirms the disciples' perseverance with him through trials and promises them seats at his table in his kingdom, judging the twelve tribes (Luke 22:28–30). He warns Simon that Satan has demanded to sift him like wheat, but Jesus has prayed that his faith may not fail; when Simon turns back, he must strengthen the brothers (Luke 22:31–32). Simon protests; Jesus predicts the threefold denial before the cock crows (Luke 22:33–34). Finally, the disciples are to now take purse, bag, and sword—the time of protection is ending; Jesus is being numbered with the transgressors (Luke 22:35–37).
24.historical_context: The table-discourse in Luke is unique in placing the greatness dispute and the servant-leadership teaching at the Last Supper (Matthew and Mark place the comparable dispute in the travel narrative). Luke's version of the farewell discourse is more extensive than the Synoptic parallels, anticipating John's long farewell discourse. The Satan-sifting image recalls Job 1:12 where the adversary is given permission to test the righteous; Jesus' intercession for Peter parallels the heavenly intercession of Romans 8:34. The two swords (v.38) are both over-interpreted (literal call to arms) and under-interpreted (purely metaphorical); most likely they are a transitional symbol signifying that the era of peaceful mission is giving way to an era of hostility.
24.literary_structure: The discourse comprises four distinct units: greatness redefined (vv.24–27), promise to the persevering disciples (vv.28–30), Simon's warning and commission (vv.31–34), and the purse/sword instruction (vv.35–38). They are united by the table setting and by the theme of preparation for what is coming—both the cross and the post-resurrection community. Jesus' servant-identity is announced at the moment he is about to give the supreme demonstration of it; Peter's future commission is given at the moment of his predicted failure—suggesting that Jesus' vision of his disciples encompasses their falls without being limited by them.
24.theological_application: The servant-leadership teaching at the Last Supper is the most powerful context for its delivery: Jesus makes the claim 'I am among you as the one who serves' at the moment he is about to serve by dying. Service is not a management technique but the shape of the Son's own life. The church's leadership structures are measured by this standard: not the authority of rank but the authority of cruciform service. Simon Peter's story—warned, denied, restored, commissioned—is the pastoral paradigm for leaders who fall: Jesus' prayer precedes the failure, his restoration follows it, and the commission to strengthen others is given through the experience of personal weakness.
24.christology: Jesus' claim to be 'among you as the one who serves' (Luke 22:27) is a compressed christology of the incarnation: the eternal Son has taken the servant's position among human beings, and this position is not temporary condescension but the revelation of God's true character. His prayer for Peter's faith—'I have prayed for you that your faith may not fail' (Luke 22:32)—is an act of priestly intercession that anticipates the intercessory work of the risen Christ at the Father's right hand. The 'numbered with transgressors' citation from Isaiah 53:12 (v.37) is the only place in the Synoptics where Jesus explicitly identifies himself with the Servant of Isaiah 53.

39.pericope_label: Jesus Prays on the Mount of Olives
39.synthesis: Jesus goes to the Mount of Olives as was his custom, with the disciples following (Luke 22:39). He tells them to pray not to enter into temptation, withdraws about a stone's throw, and kneels—a posture unique to Luke—praying: 'Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me. Nevertheless, not my will, but yours, be done' (Luke 22:41–42). Luke records that an angel appears to strengthen him, and he prays more earnestly, his sweat becoming like drops of blood (Luke 22:43–44). He returns to find the disciples sleeping from sorrow and repeats the command to pray against entering temptation (Luke 22:45–46).
39.historical_context: The Mount of Olives east of Jerusalem was a place of prayer and eschatological significance (Zechariah 14:4); Gethsemane at its base (an olive oil press) gave the episode its Synoptic name. Luke does not name Gethsemane, using 'the place' as if familiar to his audience. The angel's strengthening and the sweat like blood (vv.43–44, possibly secondary in the manuscript tradition but present in most versions) are unique to Luke and may reflect Luke's medical interest or an alternate tradition. 'From sorrow' as the reason for the disciples' sleep (v.45) is also Lukan—compassionate toward the disciples in a way that Mark's version is not. The 'cup' is a standard OT metaphor for divine judgment or suffering (Psalm 75:8; Isaiah 51:17).
39.literary_structure: The prayer is framed by two identical commands to the disciples: 'Pray that you may not enter into temptation' (vv.40, 46). This bracketing makes Jesus' own prayer the content that defines what faithful prayer in the face of temptation looks like—full disclosure of the soul's desire, total submission to the Father's will, sustained through anguish by divine strengthening. The angel's ministry (v.43) picks up the thread of Luke 4:13 where the tempter 'departed until an opportune time'; the opportune time has arrived, and the Son's response is not capitulation but the deepest obedience.
39.theological_application: The Gethsemane prayer is the model of prayer in extremis: honest before God about the soul's desire ('remove this cup'), submitted to the Father's will ('not my will but yours'), sustained by divine strength (the angel), and not abandoned to isolation in the darkest moment. The disciples' sleeping from sorrow is not primarily moral failure but human limitation—grief has overwhelmed their capacity for watchful prayer. The instruction to pray against entering temptation is the pastoral word: the disciples will face their own form of the same test in hours; prayer is the preparation Jesus models and recommends.
39.christology: Jesus' prayer in Gethsemane is among the most intimate christological texts in the NT: the Son addresses the Father with the full weight of genuine desire for relief from the cross, yet subordinates that desire to the Father's will. This is not performance but the depth of the hypostatic union—the human will of the Son genuinely recoiling from suffering, and the divine will genuinely embracing the Father's purpose, held together in the same person. 'Not my will but yours' is the supreme expression of the Son's eternal obedience, enacted in the darkest hour. The angel who strengthens him is the Father's provision for the Son's humanity in the moment of ultimate testing.

47.synthesis: While Jesus is still speaking, Judas arrives with a crowd; he approaches to kiss Jesus. Jesus stops him: 'Judas, would you betray the Son of Man with a kiss?' (Luke 22:47–48). The disciples ask whether to strike with swords; one cuts off the high priest's servant's ear (Luke 22:49–50). Jesus touches and heals the ear—the last healing in Luke before the resurrection (Luke 22:51). To the chief priests and officers who have come, Jesus says: 'This is your hour, and the power of darkness' (Luke 22:53).
47.historical_context: The kiss of greeting (philēma) was a conventional sign of respect between student and teacher; its use as the signal of betrayal is the inversion of the relationship's most basic symbol. Jesus' naming of Judas ('Judas, would you betrayal the Son of Man with a kiss?') is not in the other Synoptics and reflects Luke's tendency toward direct address. The healing of the high priest's servant's ear (Luke 22:51) is unique to Luke, consistent with his portrait of Jesus as healer even in the moment of his own arrest, and dramatically reverses the disciples' violent act. 'This is your hour, and the power of darkness' (v.53) echoes John 12:27's 'now is the judgment of this world' and locates the arrest within the cosmic conflict initiated in Luke 22:3.
47.literary_structure: The arrest scene moves quickly through three moments: the betrayal-kiss (vv.47–48), the disciples' violent resistance and Jesus' healing reversal (vv.49–51), and Jesus' address to the arresting party (vv.52–53). Each moment enacts a reversal: the sign of friendship becomes betrayal; the disciples' sword-strike is healed by the one being arrested; the apparent triumph of the authorities is named as 'the power of darkness.' The three reversals together present the arrest not as Jesus' defeat but as a moment in which his character continues to express itself even in the most adverse circumstances.
47.theological_application: The healing of the servant's ear at the moment of arrest is the final demonstration of Jesus' consistent character: mercy extended even toward the instruments of opposition. The disciples' instinct to defend with swords is stopped—the kingdom does not advance by violence, and the pattern of the passion is one of non-retaliation and submission to the Father's will. 'The power of darkness' (v.53) is a frank acknowledgment that what is happening is spiritually evil—yet it serves divine purpose. The church must hold together the reality that evil operates within God's sovereign purposes without making God the author of evil or neutralizing human and demonic responsibility.
47.christology: Jesus' naming of Judas ('would you betray the Son of Man with a kiss?') is simultaneously an indictment and an appeal—even at the moment of betrayal, Jesus addresses Judas directly, personally, without violence. The healing of the servant's ear is the last creative act of the one who came not to destroy but to save (Luke 9:56). 'The power of darkness' being named as the arresting party's true authority frames the passion as the Son of Man's hour of apparent defeat that is simultaneously the decisive battle in which darkness overreaches and is ultimately overcome at the resurrection.

54.synthesis: Jesus is arrested and led to the high priest's house. Peter follows at a distance (Luke 22:54). In the courtyard by the fire, three people identify him as Jesus' associate; three times he denies: 'I do not know what you are talking about'; 'I am not'; 'I do not know what you are saying' (Luke 22:57–60). While he is still speaking, the rooster crows. The Lord turns and looks at Peter (Luke 22:61)—a detail unique to Luke. Peter remembers the word and goes out and weeps bitterly (Luke 22:62).
54.historical_context: The high priest's house (the residence of Caiaphas) would have had an enclosed courtyard where servants and followers could gather around a fire during the cool spring night. The detail of the rooster crowing is precise: the Roman night was divided into watches, and the 'cockcrow' watch was the third night watch (roughly midnight to 3 a.m.). Luke's unique detail—'the Lord turned and looked at Peter' (v.61)—implies that Jesus was within sight of the courtyard during his interrogation, a spatial proximity that makes the look devastating. The scene intercuts with Jesus' mistreatment in the house (vv.63–65), creating a parallel narrative of two trials: Peter's in the courtyard, Jesus' inside.
54.literary_structure: The three denials are structured as escalating pressure: a servant girl by the fire (v.56), another person (v.58), and a third insistent questioner (v.59). The third denial is the most emphatic ('I do not know what you are saying') and is immediately followed by the rooster's crow. Luke's insertion of 'the Lord turned and looked at Peter' between the crow and Peter's remembrance makes the look the emotional climax—not accusation but recognition, the moment where the predicted word is confirmed and the relationship remains. Peter's bitter weeping (v.62) is the fruit of the look: sorrow that opens toward restoration, not hopeless guilt.
54.theological_application: Peter's denial is the pastoral text of every disciple's failure under pressure. He does not plan to deny; he follows, he warms himself at the fire, he is caught by incremental social pressure. The three denials are not one bold apostasy but three small capitulations that accumulate into the same result. The look of Jesus—turning to meet Peter's eyes across the courtyard—is the moment of grace at the heart of the judgment: the one being denied still holds the denier in his gaze. This is the love that will restore Peter before breakfast on the beach (John 21:15–17). The church's pastoral care for those who have failed must be shaped by this look.
54.christology: The look of Jesus across the courtyard is a christological moment: the one being mocked, beaten, and tried indoors simultaneously holds the broken disciple in compassionate gaze outdoors. This simultaneous suffering-and-caring is the portrait of the shepherd who endures attack while never losing sight of the sheep. Peter had said he would follow Jesus to prison and death (Luke 22:33); Jesus had said he had prayed for Peter's faith not to fail, and that when Peter turned back, he would strengthen the brothers (Luke 22:32). The look across the courtyard is the look of one whose prayer for Peter has not been abandoned by Peter's failure—the intercessor holds what the disciple cannot hold alone.

63.synthesis: The men holding Jesus mock and beat him, blindfolding him and demanding he prophesy who struck him (Luke 22:63–65). At daybreak, the council of elders assembles and questions him: 'If you are the Christ, tell us.' Jesus replies: 'If I tell you, you will not believe, and if I ask you, you will not answer. But from now on the Son of Man shall be seated at the right hand of the power of God' (Luke 22:66–69). They ask: 'Are you the Son of God then?' He answers: 'You say that I am.' They declare no further testimony is needed—he has condemned himself (Luke 22:70–71).
63.historical_context: Luke's account of the Sanhedrin hearing differs from Matthew and Mark by placing it at daybreak rather than during the night—consistent with proper legal procedure (Jewish law required capital trials to begin in the daytime). The Sanhedrin (council of elders, chief priests, and scribes, v.66) was the Jewish governing council with authority over internal religious matters; the Romans retained the right of capital punishment, which is why the case must go to Pilate. Jesus' 'from now on' (apo tou nyn, v.69) is a Lukan formula for inaugurated eschatology—from this moment forward, the Son of Man's exaltation has begun, even as he stands accused. The phrase combines Psalm 110:1 (right hand of power) with Daniel 7:13 (Son of Man), which the council reads as blasphemy.
63.literary_structure: The mockery (vv.63–65) is ironic: the men mock Jesus as a false prophet by asking him to prophesy, while he is simultaneously predicting his exaltation—and both the mock prophecy and the real one are being fulfilled in real time. The council questioning divides into two: are you the Christ? (vv.67–68) and are you the Son of God? (vv.70–71). Jesus answers both by pointing to the Son of Man's enthronement—an answer that is simultaneously more and other than they expected. 'You say that I am' (v.70) is a Greek idiom for a guarded affirmation: yes, but the categories you are using are inadequate.
63.theological_application: The trial before the council exposes the mechanism by which religious power condemns truth: they call it blasphemy for Jesus to claim what is simply true about himself. The church's own history contains examples of the same pattern—councils condemning prophetic voices whose vision exceeds institutional categories. The call to the church from this passage is to test its own condemnations: is the accused speaking truth that the institution is not ready to receive, or genuine error? Jesus' quiet persistence—answering without self-defense, pointing to his exaltation even in the moment of condemnation—is the model of prophetic witness before hostile authority.
63.christology: 'From now on the Son of Man shall be seated at the right hand of the power of God' (Luke 22:69) is the most direct claim to divine enthronement in Luke's passion narrative. Jesus announces his glorification in the moment of his deepest humiliation—the exaltation is not a reversal of the passion but its proper resolution, begun from 'now on.' The council's verdict (blasphemy) is the human judgment; the divine verdict will be the resurrection. The mocking guards who demand prophecy from the blindfolded Jesus are unwittingly enacting the fulfillment of his prophecy: rejected, handed to the authorities, condemned—all of which he predicted, and all of which is proceeding according to the Father's plan.
